from Iowa State College in 1928, he joined the agircultural education

ing and later as professor and head of agricultural education. In 1931 he
became a supervisor on the California Bureau of Agricultural Education

In 1945 he was instrumental in starting teacher education in
homemaking on the Davis campus, and later started programs of teacher
- education for both elementary and secondary schcol teachers. These
programs are characterized by the high standards and quality which he
developed in the program for the preparation of teachers of agriculture.
The Distinguished Service Award candidates for 1963 were selected
on the basis of their contribution in the following areas:
1. Excellence in teaching undergraduate or graduate students
or both.
2. Significant rescarch in the. field of agricultural
education and general education.
3. Scholarly writing pertaining to the field of education.

_ Following is a brief resume of Professor Sutherland's activities,
presented by his colleagues to suprort the statement that he is a
A_methods:
master teacher, a forceful leader, a talented writer, and a competent
researcher.
Excellence in Teaching
In 1932 Professor Cutherland initiated the cadet program of teacher
-23-
education in agriculture on the caunrus of the University of California,
Tavis. This progrum is still ir om ration in much the same form as originally conceived. In the past 31 years, 527 men have completed this program
and have received credentials as teachers of vocational agriculture.
The high tenure of these teachers speaks for the quality of this program
of preparation; 45 per cent of this group are still in teaching and less
than 5 per cent are in non-agricultural rositions; the others are in
college or university teaching, school administration, state supervisory
positions, or in related agriculture.
Professor Sutherland is recognized locally and in the state and
nation as a master teacher. Evidence of his ability as a teacher is
his popularity as a discussion leader and conference director. During
the past ten years, there has been hardly a month that he has not directed
at least one conference or led a discussion group or talked before a professional or service group. He receives these invitations because he is
recognized as an outstanding speaker, leader and educator. For example,
early in September, 1962, he was invited by the Agricultural and Mechanical
College of Texas to direct a leadership workshop for student body officers
of 6 large colleges and universities in Texas. In October of that year,
A_results:
he led a 2-day workshop for a group of 20 teachers who are developing
experimental programs in the teaching of biological principles in vocational
‘agriculture. In the following December, he conducted a workshop at
‘Columbus, Ohio for teachers developing similar programs in vocational agriculture. In May, 1663, he conducted a workshop for staff members of Oregon
State University on using the principle approach to the teaching of agriculture, In addition, he played a key role in directing the annual
planning conference for the Culifornia Bureau of Agricultural Education.
He is on the program for the Annual California Agricultural Teachers
Conference at San Luis Obisrpo, California,
Research
In addition to being Chairman of the Department of Agricultural
Education at the University of California, Frefessor Sutherland has
conducted imaginative research, centering primarily on the improvement of vocational agriculture. He was one of the first leaders in
the country to study the training requirements of workers in agricultural business. His initial study, completed in 1957, has gained
nationwide attenticn and has served as a model for many communities
and states interested in this problem. In 1960 he started a chollenging study to identify the biological principles related to agriculture
and to find how these could be incorporated into courses of high
school vocational agriculture. This study has had liberal support
from the Nationvl Defense Fducation Act funds. He is now in the
second year of field experimentation with pilot programs in 16
high schools. Preliminary results are rost promising. This study
has likewise generated nationwide interest. It promises to make
. the greatest contribution to the upgrading of instruction in vocational agriculture that has been accomplished in the fast decade.
Writings
Professor Sutherland has made a major contribution to his yrefession through his writing. One of his books, When You Preside,
-2he
< has become a classic in the field. It is used as a textbook in many colleges,
"and is also used extensively by members of the Agricultural Extension fervice,
service clubs, church groups, fraternal organizations, and many others. His
- wanual on Biological Principles in Agriculture, is a "first" in its field.
“: Jt promises to have a very strong influence on upgrading the scientific
aspect of the course content in vocational agriculture. In addition to
. articles in professional journals and magazines, he is the author of several bulletins and ramphlets.
Service Rendered
The record of service of Professor Sutherland is likewise outstanding. A listing of committees and other activities on which he
has served is an inadequate measure of his contribution to the profession. The services rendered by Professor Sutherland number in
’- the dozens end cover a wide range of activities. The wise counsel
and inspiration he gives his students and co-workers, added to his
.- formal service, are characteristics that make Professor Sutherland
_ dear to his students and "father” to them all.
